title: Severe Preeclampsia with HELLP Syndrome
subtitle: 32 y/o G2P1 at 34 weeks, sudden RUQ pain and severe HTN
nursesNote: 0530 - 32 y/o G2P1 at 34 weeks gestation admitted overnight from clinic for elevated BP (162/104) and 3+ proteinuria. Reports new-onset severe right upper quadrant pain rated 7/10, persistent frontal headache unrelieved by acetaminophen, and 'spots' in her vision since 0400. Brisk 4+ deep tendon reflexes with 2 beats of clonus bilaterally. Mild facial and pretibial edema noted. Fundus measures appropriate for dates; FHR 142 with moderate variability and no decelerations on continuous EFM. Magnesium sulfate bolus and maintenance ordered; betamethasone 12 mg IM given for fetal lung maturity; OB and MFM at bedside discussing delivery timing. Patient anxious, asking 'Is my baby okay?'

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) cues that BEST establish HELLP syndrome superimposed on severe preeclampsia.

opts.text: Platelets 82 K/uL with AST 186 and LDH 742
opts.rat: HELLP triad: Hemolysis (LDH >600), Elevated Liver enzymes (AST/ALT >2x normal), Low Platelets (<100K). All three present here - definitive HELLP diagnosis (ACOG 2020).

opts.text: RUQ pain 7/10 with persistent frontal headache and visual disturbances ('spots')
opts.rat: RUQ pain reflects hepatic capsular stretch from periportal hemorrhage - a hallmark HELLP sign. Headache and visual changes signal CNS involvement and impending eclampsia. These are the 'severe features' that mandate delivery.

opts.text: BP escalating from 168/108 to 182/118 with brisk 4+ DTRs and clonus
opts.rat: Severe-range BP (>=160/110) plus hyperreflexia with clonus indicates CNS irritability and high seizure risk. This is severe preeclampsia by ACOG criteria; combined with HELLP, delivery is indicated regardless of gestational age.

opts.text: FHR 142 with moderate variability and no decelerations
opts.rat: This is a REASSURING finding indicating fetal well-being - not a HELLP cue. Important to monitor but does not establish maternal pathology.

opts.text: Mild pretibial and facial edema
opts.rat: Edema alone is no longer diagnostic for preeclampsia (was removed from criteria years ago). Many normal pregnancies have edema.

opts.text: 32 y/o G2P1 at 34 weeks gestation
opts.rat: Demographic and gestational data - context, not a cue. Does not establish disease.

opts.text: Magnesium level 5.8 mg/dL (therapeutic range)
opts.rat: This is a TREATMENT response, not a disease cue. Therapeutic magnesium is the goal of seizure prophylaxis, not a sign of HELLP.

opts.text: 24-hour urine protein 4.8 g
opts.rat: Confirms the diagnosis of preeclampsia (>0.3 g) but does NOT establish HELLP specifically. HELLP is defined by the H, E, and L laboratory criteria.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) statements that correctly link the cues to the underlying pathophysiology.

opts.text: Abnormal placentation triggers widespread endothelial dysfunction, causing vasospasm, hypertension, and multi-organ ischemia - delivery of the placenta is the only definitive cure
opts.rat: Preeclampsia is fundamentally a placental disease. Until the placenta is delivered, the cascade continues. This is why delivery is the ultimate treatment regardless of gestational age in severe disease (ACOG).

opts.text: Microangiopathic hemolytic anemia from RBCs being sheared as they pass through fibrin-narrowed vessels causes the elevated LDH and dropping hemoglobin
opts.rat: Schistocytes on peripheral smear and rising LDH reflect mechanical RBC destruction - the 'H' in HELLP. This explains the anemia (Hgb 9.8) without obvious bleeding.

opts.text: Hepatic sinusoidal obstruction by fibrin deposits causes periportal necrosis, capsular distension, and the characteristic RUQ pain - subcapsular hematoma rupture is a feared complication
opts.rat: RUQ/epigastric pain in HELLP signals hepatic stretching. Spontaneous liver rupture is rare but catastrophic - any sudden worsening of pain or hemodynamic instability warrants emergent imaging.

opts.text: Magnesium sulfate works as an antihypertensive by blocking calcium channels in vascular smooth muscle
opts.rat: Magnesium is a SEIZURE PROPHYLACTIC, not an antihypertensive. It reduces eclamptic seizures by ~50% but has minimal effect on BP. Labetalol or hydralazine are used for severe HTN.

opts.text: Proteinuria is caused by glomerular damage from hypertension itself, with no relationship to the placental pathology
opts.rat: Backwards. The same endothelial dysfunction that causes HTN also damages the glomerular endothelium - both are downstream of the placental disease.

opts.text: Fetal distress is the primary trigger for delivery in HELLP, not maternal status
opts.rat: MATERNAL deterioration drives delivery in HELLP - the fetus is reassuring here. Delivering for maternal indication even with reassuring fetal status is a classic NCLEX concept.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.inst: Drag to rank the differentials from MOST likely (1) to LEAST likely (5).

opts.text: HELLP syndrome with severe preeclampsia
opts.rat: All three HELLP criteria present plus severe-range BP, severe features (headache, visual changes, RUQ pain), 4+ DTRs with clonus, and 4.8 g proteinuria. Diagnostic.

opts.text: Acute fatty liver of pregnancy (AFLP)
opts.rat: Important differential - presents with RUQ pain and elevated transaminases in late pregnancy. Distinguished by hypoglycemia, profound coagulopathy, and elevated ammonia. Less likely here without those features.

opts.text: Acute viral hepatitis
opts.rat: Could explain transaminase elevation but doesn't account for thrombocytopenia, hemolysis, or severe HTN with proteinuria. Always worth ruling out with hepatitis serologies.

opts.text: Cholecystitis or symptomatic cholelithiasis
opts.rat: RUQ pain is consistent but doesn't explain the lab abnormalities or BP findings. Pregnancy increases gallstone risk so worth considering as concurrent process.

opts.text: Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP)
opts.rat: Pentad of MAHA, thrombocytopenia, AKI, fever, neuro changes. Rare and important consideration but the temporal association with pregnancy and proteinuria favor HELLP. ADAMTS13 activity differentiates.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) MOST time-critical interventions for severe preeclampsia with HELLP at 34 weeks.

opts.text: Continue IV magnesium sulfate at 2 g/hr maintenance and monitor for toxicity (DTRs, RR, urine output, magnesium levels)
opts.rat: Magnesium reduces eclamptic seizure risk by ~50% and is class I in severe preeclampsia. Therapeutic range 4-7 mg/dL. Toxicity signs: loss of DTRs (8-10), respiratory depression (12+), cardiac arrest (>15). Calcium gluconate is the antidote.

opts.text: Administer IV labetalol or hydralazine for BP >=160/110 to target 140-150/90-100
opts.rat: Severe-range HTN demands acute treatment to prevent maternal stroke. Goal is gradual reduction (not normotension) to preserve uteroplacental perfusion. ACOG recommends labetalol, hydralazine, or oral nifedipine as first-line.

opts.text: Coordinate with OB team for prompt delivery (induction or cesarean) - delivery is the definitive treatment for HELLP
opts.rat: In severe preeclampsia with HELLP at >=34 weeks, delivery is indicated regardless of cervical favorability. Continued pregnancy worsens maternal disease. Mode of delivery depends on cervical exam, fetal status, and platelet count (CACOG).

opts.text: Administer aspirin 162 mg daily for placental insufficiency
opts.rat: Low-dose aspirin is used for PREVENTION of preeclampsia in high-risk patients starting in early pregnancy - useless and contraindicated once HELLP is established (worsens bleeding risk).

opts.text: Restrict IV fluids to <80 mL/hr and avoid bolus infusions
opts.rat: Wait - this is also INDICATED. Pulmonary edema is a major HELLP/preeclampsia complication. Total intake should generally be 80-125 mL/hr. But the question asks for THREE most time-critical - delivery, magnesium, and BP control take priority over fluid management.

opts.text: Transfuse 2 units of packed RBCs prophylactically
opts.rat: Hgb 9.8 does not require transfusion. Transfuse based on bleeding, hemodynamics, or symptoms - not just lab values. Risk of transfusion reaction outweighs benefit here.

opts.text: Place a Foley catheter and start strict hourly I&O
opts.rat: Useful for monitoring but not as time-critical as magnesium, BP control, and delivery planning. Important for the second tier of care.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Drag to rank the actions from FIRST (1) to LAST (5).

opts.text: Verify magnesium infusion is running correctly, perform DTR/clonus/RR/UOP check, ensure calcium gluconate at bedside
opts.rat: FIRST - the patient is already on mag for seizure prophylaxis. Verifying correct dose and monitoring for toxicity is the highest-priority safety check. Calcium gluconate must be immediately available.

opts.text: Administer IV labetalol per protocol for BP 182/118; recheck BP in 10 minutes
opts.rat: SECOND - severe-range BP with end-organ symptoms (headache, visual changes) requires acute treatment to prevent stroke. Repeat dosing per algorithm if BP remains severe.

opts.text: Place Foley catheter, ensure 2 large-bore IV lines patent, send type and crossmatch for 2 units PRBCs
opts.rat: THIRD - prepare for potential delivery (especially cesarean) and bleeding. Strict I&O monitors for pulmonary edema and renal function.

opts.text: Continuous EFM, position in left lateral tilt, prepare for transfer to L&D suite for delivery
opts.rat: FOURTH - left lateral position improves uteroplacental perfusion. Continuous fetal monitoring is mandatory. Delivery preparation underway.

opts.text: Provide patient/family education on diagnosis, plan, signs of worsening (worsening headache, vision loss, RUQ pain, bleeding), and emotional support
opts.rat: LAST - vital but lower acuity than the immediate medical interventions. The patient has already asked about her baby - addressing her psychosocial needs is part of comprehensive care.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: After cesarean delivery and ongoing magnesium therapy, classify each postpartum finding.

opts.text: BP gradually decreased from 182/118 to 142/88 by 12 hours postpartum
opts.rat: Gradual BP normalization after delivery is the expected trajectory once the placenta is removed. Most BPs normalize within 6 weeks postpartum.

opts.text: Platelets rose from 82 to 124 K/uL by 48 hours postpartum
opts.rat: Platelet recovery typically begins within 24-48 hours of delivery and reaches normal by 7-10 days. A reliable marker of HELLP resolution.

opts.text: AST and ALT downtrending (AST 186 -> 92, ALT 164 -> 78) at 24 hours postpartum
opts.rat: Falling transaminases indicate hepatic recovery as endothelial dysfunction resolves with placental removal.

opts.text: Sudden severe RUQ pain with hypotension (BP 78/42) and dropping hemoglobin from 9.8 to 6.4 g/dL at 6 hours postpartum
opts.rat: EMERGENCY - subcapsular hepatic hematoma rupture, a feared HELLP complication. STAT abdominal imaging, type and cross 6 units, prepare for emergent surgery, alert ICU and trauma team.

opts.text: Generalized tonic-clonic seizure 12 hours postpartum despite magnesium at 2 g/hr
opts.rat: Eclamptic seizure on therapeutic magnesium - postpartum eclampsia can occur up to 6 weeks out. Protect airway, give IV magnesium bolus, check level (may need higher), rule out other causes (intracranial bleed).

opts.text: DTRs decreased to 1+ with respiratory rate 8/min and serum magnesium 9.4 mg/dL
opts.rat: Magnesium TOXICITY. STOP infusion, give calcium gluconate 1 g IV slowly, support ventilation, recheck level. Classic NCLEX concept: loss of DTRs is the EARLIEST sign of toxicity.

opts.text: Patient bonding with newborn in NICU via video, expressing breast milk, asking appropriate questions about discharge
opts.rat: Psychosocial recovery and engagement in newborn care indicate good emotional adaptation. A meaningful outcome beyond the lab values.

opts.text: Urine output 35 mL/hr (was 60 mL/hr); creatinine 1.4 (unchanged)
opts.rat: Borderline urine output and stable creatinine reflect persistent renal vasoconstriction from preeclampsia - not improving but not acutely worsening. Continue strict I&O, avoid nephrotoxins, expect gradual recovery.
